data.text: having the two nonparallel sides equal

enrichment.etymology: borrowed from Late Latin isoscelēs, borrowed from Greek isoskelḗs "having equal legs, (of a triangle) having two equal sides, (of numbers) divisible into equal parts, even," from iso-  + -skelēs, adjective derivative of skélos (neuter s-stem) "leg," going back to an Indo-European base *skel- "bent," whence also Armenian šeł "slanting, crooked"; with o-grade, Greek skoliós "bent, crooked, askew, devious"; perhaps with a velar extension Germanic *skelga-/*skelha-, whence Old English sceolh "oblique, wry," Old Frisian skilich "squinting," Old High German skelah "crooked, oblique," Old Icelandic skjalgr "wry, oblique"
